patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hi , ms. brooks . i'm dr. baker . how are you ?"] hi , dr. baker .
[Clinician asked: "is your , is your right finger hurting ?"] yes .
[Clinician asked: "okay . hey , dragon , uh , sharon brooks is a 48 year old female here for right finger pain . all right . so , tell me what happened ."] well , i was skiing over the weekend-
[Clinician asked: "okay ."] and as i was , um , coming down the hill , i tried moguls , which jumping over those big hills , i tend to get my strap caught on my finger-
and it kind of bent it back a bit .
[Clinician asked: "okay ."] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "and when did this happen ?"] it happened , uh ... that was sunday .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and have you tried anything for this or anything made it better or worse ?"] i tried , um , putting ice on it .
[Clinician asked: "okay ."] uh , and then i- i've been taking ibuprofen , but it's still very painful .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and , uh , is it worse when you bend it ? or anything make it ... so , just wh-"] yeah , movement .
[Clinician asked: "okay ."] yes .

[Clinician asked: "okay . so , it sounds like you were skiing about four about days ago and you went over a mogul and got it hyper extended or got it bent backwards a little bit , ? okay . do you have any other past medical history at all ?"] um , i have been suffering from constipation recently .
[Clinician asked: "okay . all right . and do you take ... what medicines do you take for constipation ?"] um , i've just been taking , um , mel- um ...
[Clinician asked: "miralax ?"] miralax . that's it .
[Clinician asked: "okay . miralax is sufficient ."] miralax . yes .
[Clinician asked: "and any surgeries in the past ?"] i did have my appendix taken out when i was 18 .
[Clinician asked: "okay . let's do your exam . uh , so , it's this finger right here . and does it hurt here on your , on this joint up here ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and how'bout right there ? no ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "right here ?"] that hurts .
[Clinician asked: "all right . uh , can you bend your finger for me ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "all right . and how about extend it ? all right . and can you touch your thumb with it ?"] yes .

[Clinician asked: "all right . so , on this x-ray. everything looks normal right now . uh , i do n't see any fractures . everything lines up pretty well . uh , so , your x-ray looks normal with no fractures . so , based on the x-ray and your exam , you have some tenderness right here . i think you've got a little cont"] okay .
[Clinician asked: "okay . do you have any questions ?"] no . i think that sounds good .
[Clinician asked: "okay . hey , dragon , order the medication and procedures we discussed . all right . and why do n't you come with me and we'll get you signed out ."] okay . thank you .
gold_note:
CC:

Right finger pain.

HPI:

Ms. Brooks is a 48-year-old female who presents today for an evaluation of right finger pain. She states she was skiing on Sunday and hyperextended her finger when it became caught in a strap on a jump. She has tried applying ice and taking Ibuprofen, but it is still very painful. She has pain with movement.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:

MiraLax

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:

Constipation.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:

Appendectomy.

EXAM

Examination of the right index finger shows tenderness over the distal phalanx. No tenderness over the proximal phalanx or the MP joint. Pain with flexion.

RESULTS

X-rays of the right hand show no obvious signs of fracture or bony abnormalities.

IMPRESSION

Right hand index finger contusion at the tip of the finger.

PLAN

At this point, I discussed the diagnosis and treatment options with the patient. I have recommended a splint. She will take Motrin 600 mg every 6 hours for a week. If she does not improve, she will follow up with me. All questions were answered.

gold_sections.PHYSICAL_EXAM: Examination of the right index finger shows tenderness over the distal phalanx. No tenderness over the proximal phalanx or the MP joint. Pain with flexion.
gold_sections.RESULTS: X-rays of the right hand show no obvious signs of fracture or bony abnormalities.
gold_sections.IMPRESSION: Right hand index finger contusion at the tip of the finger.
gold_sections.PLAN: At this point, I discussed the diagnosis and treatment options with the patient. I have recommended a splint. She will take Motrin 600 mg every 6 hours for a week. If she does not improve, she will follow up with me. All questions were answered.

naive.response_text:
I understand how frustrating and painful this must be for you, Sharon. Based on your exam and the X-ray results, there doesn’t appear to be any fracture, which is good news. It seems like you have a minor contusion causing the tenderness in your finger. 

To help with the pain and inflammation, continue taking ibuprofen as directed and keep icing the area. Rest your finger and avoid activities that cause discomfort. If the pain persists or worsens after a week, or if you have any further concerns, please don’t hesitate to reach out. We’ll check in again in a week to see how you’re doing. Wishing you a speedy recovery. Thank you for coming in.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your experience, Sharon. It sounds like the skiing accident was quite stressful, and I appreciate your efforts in managing the pain with ice and ibuprofen. I understand how frustrating it must be when the pain persists despite these measures.

Based on the exam and X-ray results, it seems there's no fracture, which is good news. However, the tenderness suggests a possible sprain or soft tissue injury. This can sometimes take a bit longer to heal, even without a fracture. I recommend continuing to rest and elevate your finger, and if needed, consider a splint to keep it immobilized. We can also explore other pain management options if the ibuprofen isn't providing enough relief.

Let's plan to follow up in about two weeks to reassess your finger's condition. Before you leave, could you summarize what we discussed today? This will help ensure you're clear on the next steps and any recommendations. Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or if the pain worsens in the meantime.
